description_long: This object is a classic chessboard set on a square wooden platform, featuring a checkered playing surface with alternating light and dark squares. The board is bordered by a thick, dark brown wooden frame with a visible wood grain texture, giving it a sturdy and elegant appearance. On the board, there are two sets of chess pieces: one set is a creamy off-white color, and the other is a matte black, both with a slightly rough, hand-carved look. The chess pieces include kings, queens, bishops, knights, rooks, and pawns, each with distinct, recognizable shapes. The playing surface itself has a slightly worn, matte finish, with the light squares appearing beige and the dark squares a muted brown. The overall design is traditional, suitable for use in parks, public squares, or urban recreational areas.
description: A traditional wooden chessboard with a checkered pattern and two full sets of hand-carved chess pieces in off-white and black, bordered by a thick, textured wooden frame.
description_view_0: Front view showing the chessboard with both sets of pieces lined up, highlighting the thick wooden border and the alternating squares.
description_view_1: Side view focusing on the arrangement of the chess pieces and the depth of the wooden frame.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the back row of chess pieces and the full width of the board's border.
description_view_3: Opposite side view emphasizing the off-white pieces and the detailed texture of the board's surface.
attribute_board_pattern: The playing surface features a classic checkered pattern composed of alternating light beige and dark brown squares, arranged in a regular grid. The squares are evenly sized and have a slightly worn, textured appearance, with no additional decorative elements or markings within the grid.
attribute_border_design: The border is a thick, dark brown wooden frame with a visible wood grain texture, approximately 2-3 centimeters wide, and has a simple, unadorned design with no inscriptions or decorative markings.
